feed.title: K-of-N threshhold key generation scheme by limited shared secrets

entry.title: K-of-N threshhold key generation scheme by limited shared secrets
entry.summary:
The query raises an important comparison between using certain cryptographic methods, specifically contrasting the merits of Pseudo-random secret sharing or Replicated secret sharing against other techniques such as those outlined in [Arctic](https://eprint.iacr.org/2024/466). It specifically questions the necessity and efficiency of alternative methodologies similar to the [BLISK](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/blisk-boolean-circuit-logic-integrated-into-the-single-key/2217/15) concept. BLISK integrates Boolean circuit logic into a single key system but notably lacks Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKPs), which are often considered crucial for enhancing security protocols in cryptographic systems.
The inquiry implies skepticism about adopting new frameworks that omit ZKPs, suggesting that existing approaches like replicated secret sharing might already provide adequate security and efficiency without the added complexity or potential overhead of newer systems that exclude ZKP technology. This discussion points towards a broader evaluation of whether the innovation in cryptographic techniques, particularly those that skip fundamental components like ZKPs, justifies their adoption over more traditional, possibly simpler methods. The implication here is that while innovation is valuable, it is equally important to assess whether newer methods offer significant improvements or advantages over established practices in practical scenarios.
